brief:
GAP/RATIONALE: Cryoballoon (CB) PVI is established for paroxysmal AF (PAF), but recurrence still occurs; role of individual PV anatomy/ostial size in predicting long-term arrhythmia-free survival after single-shot CB-guided PVI is unclear. Aim: determine whether anatomical PV characteristics predict long-term freedom from AF recurrence.

DESIGN/SETTING: Single-center observational cohort study; consecutive patients undergoing CB-guided PVI for PAF between 2012 and 2018. Preprocedural cardiac MRI used to assess individual PV anatomy; cross-sectional area (CSA) computed per PV.

POPULATION/N: 353 consecutive PAF patients; mean age 58 +/- 11 years; 56% male.

INTERVENTION/COMPARISON: Single-shot CB-guided PVI (no separate comparator arm); comparison by anatomical subgroups (normal vs variant PV anatomy; presence vs absence of left common PV [LCPV] and right variant PVs) and by enlarged vs normal PV CSA.

KEY RESULTS (exact numbers):
- Acute PVI achieved in 100% of patients.
- Normal PV anatomy (2 left + 2 right PVs): 223 patients (63%).
- Variant PV anatomy: 130 patients (37%).
- Observation period: 48 months.
- AF recurrence documented in 167 patients (47%).
- Patients with AF recurrence had significantly enlarged right-sided PVs and left superior PVs (LSPVs), p < 0.001.
- Left common PVs (LCPVs) present in n = 75; associated with significantly impaired long-term AF-free survival vs normal anatomy (Log-rank p < 0.001).
- Right variant PVs present in n = 35; associated with significantly impaired long-term AF-free survival vs normal anatomy (Log-rank p < 0.001).

MAIN TAKEAWAY: Variant PV anatomy is a good predictor of AF recurrence after CB-guided PVI for PAF. Enlarged CSA of right-sided PVs and of LSPVs correlates with AF recurrence.
